evidence.title: Effects of Black Soy Peptide Supplementation on Blood Pressure and Oxidative Stress:A Randomized Controlled Trial
evidence.snippet: Title: Effects of Black Soy Peptide Supplementation on Blood Pressure and Oxidative Stress:A Randomized Controlled Trial

evidence.title: Brief Summary
evidence.snippet: Black soy peptides have been shown to possess properties that may decrease blood pressure. To examine the effects of black soy peptides supplementation on blood pressure and oxidative stress in subjects with pre-hypertension or stage I hypertension.

evidence.snippet: Status: COMPLETED

evidence.snippet: DIETARY_SUPPLEMENT: black soy peptide - black soy peptide: 4.5g/day for 8 weeks

evidence.snippet:
Title: Black soy peptide supplementation improves glucose control in subjects with prediabetes and newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes mellitus.
Authors: Kwak Jung Hyun, Lee Jong Ho, Ahn Chang-Won et al.
Journal: Journal of medicinal food (2010)
Abstract: The present study aimed to determine the effect of black soy peptide supplementation on glucose control in subjects with prediabetes (impaired fasting glucose or impaired glucose tolerance) and newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes mellitus (DM). evidence.title: Weight reduction effects of a black soy peptide supplement in overweight and obese subjects: double blind, randomized, controlled study.
evidence.snippet:
Title: Weight reduction effects of a black soy peptide supplement in overweight and obese subjects: double blind, randomized, controlled study.
Authors: Kwak Jung Hyun, Ahn Chang-Won, Park Soo-Hyun et al.
Journal: Food & function (2012)
Abstract: The present study determined the effect of black soy peptide supplementation on body weight and body fat in overweight/obese subjects. In a double-blind controlled trial, participants (n = 80) were randomized to either soy peptide supplementation (the test group) or to a placebo (the placebo group).

evidence.title: Novel black soy peptides with antiobesity effects: activation of leptin-like signaling and AMP-activated protein kinase.
evidence.snippet:
Title: Novel black soy peptides with antiobesity effects: activation of leptin-like signaling and AMP-activated protein kinase.
Authors: Jang E-H, Moon J-S, Ko J H et al.
Journal: International journal of obesity (2005) (2008)
Abstract: OBJECTIVE: To investigate the mechanisms underlying the antiobesity effects of a novel isoflavone-free peptide mixture (BSP) derived from black soybean. The provided information indicates that the intervention in this trial is a dietary supplement containing black soy peptides, which are not classified as pharmaceutical peptides used for therapeutic purposes. Therefore, based on the critical rules outlined, the primary intervention here does not qualify as a peptide therapeutic.

According to the rules provided, if the active drug is not explicitly stated to be a peptide, and it is instead a nutritional formula or dietary supplement, then it is classified as False. In this case, Black Soy Peptide is a dietary supplement derived from black soybeans, not a peptide therapeutic.

While the black soy peptide has biological activity, its mechanism of action involves binding to somatostatin receptors, which are involved in hormone regulation rather than direct antimicrobial activity. The trial's focus on conditions like hypertension and prediabetes further supports this classification.

These findings indicate that the trial met its primary endpoints, making it a positive trial.

Instead, the studies found improvements in glucose control, weight reduction, and activation of leptin-like signaling and AMP-activated protein kinase, but not in blood pressure or oxidative stress as intended.

identificationModule.briefTitle: Effects of Black Soy Peptide Supplementation on Blood Pressure
identificationModule.officialTitle: Effects of Black Soy Peptide Supplementation on Blood Pressure and Oxidative Stress:A Randomized Controlled Trial

descriptionModule.briefSummary: Black soy peptides have been shown to possess properties that may decrease blood pressure. To examine the effects of black soy peptides supplementation on blood pressure and oxidative stress in subjects with pre-hypertension or stage I hypertension.
descriptionModule.detailedDescription: Trial participants included men and women aged 30 to 65 years who had an average systolic blood pressure (SBP) between 130 to 159 mmHg, average diastolic blood pressure (DBP) between 80 to 99 mmHg, or both, based on an average of six measurements during two screening visits. Participants were supplied with 84 pouches of placebo (casein) or black soy peptide (3 pouches/day) at 0-week and at 4-week visits. Test group subjects received pouches containing black soy peptides (4.5 g/day total soy peptides for 8 weeks). The control group received pouches containing casein that had a similar appearance to the black soy peptide tablet. During the intervention, we instructed study participants to continue their current food intake patterns and lifestyles so that total energy intake and energy expenditure would be constant during the course of trial. Participants brought back unconsumed pouches at their 4- and 8-week follow-up visits. The dietitian counted the number of returned pouches, and we used this to assess the participants' adherence to their assigned intervention.

armGroups.label: placebo
armGroups.description: similar appearance to the black soy peptide tablet, 8 weeks

primaryOutcomes.measure: change from baseline in Systolic blood pressure at 8 weeks

secondaryOutcomes.measure: Change from baseline in Diastolic blood pressure at 8 weeks
change from baseline in plasma malondialdehyde at 8 weeks
change from baseline in Urinary 8-epi-prostaglandin F2 at 8 weeks
change from baseline in Renin at 8 weeks
Change from baseline in angiotensin-converting enzyme at 8 weeks

eligibilityModule.eligibilityCriteria:
Inclusion Criteria:

* Average systolic blood pressure (SBP) between 130 to 159 mmHg
* Average diastolic blood pressure (DBP) between 80 to 99 mmHg, or both, based on an average of six measurements during two screening visits

Exclusion Criteria:

* Previous diagnosed clinical hypertension
* Self-reported use of anti-hypertensive medication
* Abnormal liver or renal function
* History of cardiovascular disease, cancer, thyroid or pituitary disease, or any other serious life-threatening illness that required regular medical treatment
